text: The surgeon enters the abdominal cavity using open laparoscopy and places two 5mm trocars in the right and left lumbar regions, along with a 12mm port in the left hypochondrium.

The surgeon releases adhesions in the supraumbilical midline, which are visibly related to the patient's previous laparotomies.

The surgeon explores the upper quadrants, identifying and dissecting epiploic adhesions attached to the liver.

The surgeon opens the remnants of the small omentum, revealing the cecum visibly occupying the left upper quadrant, positioned in front of the stomach.
The surgeon opens the remnants of the small omentum, revealing the cecum positioned in the left upper quadrant, anterior to the stomach.
The surgeon performs maneuvers to reduce the hernia by gently retracting the column from the right side and pushing the cecum from the left side.

The surgeon performs maneuvers to reduce the hernia by gently retracting the column from the right side while simultaneously pushing the cecum from the left side, aiming to reposition the herniated structures.
The surgeon successfully reduces the colon, revealing the anatomy of the foramen of Winslow for further exploration.

The anatomical structures are visualized with the area located anterior to the inferior vena cava, posterior to the hepatoduodenal ligament, and surrounded by the caudate lobe of the liver.
The anatomical structure is located anterior to the inferior vena cava, posterior to the hepatoduodenal ligament, and is surrounded by the caudate lobe of the liver.
The video shows the foramen of Winslow, which appears narrow, suggesting that the herniation is caused by abnormal mobility of the ascending colon.
The surgeon observes the foramen of Winslow, noting its narrow width, and identifies the abnormal mobility of the ascending colon as the cause of the herniation.
The surgeon visually assesses the herniated intestinal segment, confirming its viability with no signs of compromised blood flow or tissue damage.

The surgeon places the cecum in its orthoptic position and begins the ileocolopexy procedure. The mesocolon is secured with multiple cross-stitches of non-absorbable suture, extending up to the right colic angle.

The surgeon irrigates the abdominal cavity, ensuring thorough cleansing of the surgical area following the reduction of the colon.
The surgeon irrigates the abdominal cavity, ensuring thorough washing of the area.
The surgical field shows no active bleeding, indicating that hemostasis is not required at this stage of the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon packs the foramen of Winslow with diamantum to prevent recurrences, ensuring the closure of the herniation site.
